ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Dal Makhani. FOODON_03311737 (lentil food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0DPX (Lentils, cooked) is the closest parent. No specific Dal Makhani entry exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 229961001 (Lentil substance) is the closest available anchor. No specific Dal Makhani concept exists in SNOMED CT.

serving.natural_language_portion: A standard restaurant serving of Dal Makhani is approximately 250 g, typically served as a main dish or side with rice or bread.

nutrient_source.title: Dal Makhani — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (Revised Edition 2017), Table: Cooked Legume Dishes; supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for cooked black lentils (FDC ID 175254) for micronutrient detail
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN); USDA Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides the primary macro and sodium values for Dal Makhani as a cooked dish. Micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, iron, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc) are supplemented from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for cooked black lentils (FDC ID 175254) and adjusted proportionally for the butter/cream enrichment typical of restaurant-style Dal Makhani. Values represent a typical restaurant preparation with butter and cream.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition; Black beans / whole lentils cooked entry used as analogous estimate for Dal Makhani
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Dal Makhani in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature. GI of 29 is estimated from the closest analogous entry: whole cooked black lentils / urad dal (GI ~29, glucose reference), as reported in Atkinson et al. 2021 International GI Tables. The butter and cream enrichment in Dal Makhani further suppresses glycemic response due to fat-mediated gastric emptying delay, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (29 × 31.25) / 100 ≈ 9.1 for a 250 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g; ~800 mg per restaurant serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Dal Makhani to small portions (≤150 g) and request reduced salt preparation, as a standard 250 g restaurant serving contributes approximately 800 mg sodium — about 35% of the recommended daily limit of 2300 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~29) and high dietary fibre (3.2 g per 100 g) from whole black lentils
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dal Makhani has a low glycemic index (~29) and a moderate glycemic load (~9 per serving), making it a suitable legume-based dish for people with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in standard portions; however, the butter and cream content adds saturated fat and calories that should be factored into overall dietary planning.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from butter and cream (~3.1 g per 100 g; ~7.8 g per serving) alongside beneficial dietary fibre and plant protein
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: While Dal Makhani provides heart-beneficial dietary fibre and plant protein from black lentils, the saturated fat contributed by butter and cream (~7.8 g per 250 g serving) approaches 39% of the recommended daily saturated fat limit of 20 g, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should request reduced-butter preparations or limit portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dal Makhani is a classic North Indian slow-cooked legume dish originating from the Punjab region of India. It is prepared by simmering whole black lentils (urad dal) and red kidney beans (rajma) overnight or for many hours — traditionally in a tandoor or over a low flame — until the lentils become exceptionally soft and creamy. The dish is finished with a tempering (tadka) of butter, cream, tomatoes, onion, ginger, garlic, cumin, coriander, garam masala, and chilli, which gives it a rich, velvety texture and a deep, smoky, mildly spiced flavour. The 'Khaas' (special) designation in restaurant menus typically indicates a chef's premium preparation using house-blend masala and extended cooking time. Dal Makhani is a staple of North Indian restaurant menus worldwide and is widely served in Singapore's Indian restaurants. Nutritionally, it provides moderate protein (~5.8 g per 100 g), dietary fibre (~3.2 g per 100 g), and iron (~2.1 mg per 100 g) from the lentils, alongside saturated fat and calories from the butter and cream enrichment. Its low glycemic index (~29) makes it suitable for blood glucose management, though the sodium content (~320 mg per 100 g) warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension. It is vegetarian, halal, and gluten-free, making it broadly accessible across dietary requirements.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Dal Makhani?
Is Dal Makhani good for diabetics?
Is Dal Makhani high in protein?
What lentils are used in Dal Makhani?
What is the GI of Dal Makhani?

confidence_reason: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) provides the closest approved primary source for Dal Makhani as a cooked Indian legume dish. Macro values are adapted from the ICMR-NIN Dal Makhani entry and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for micronutrients. Confidence is medium rather than high because the exact restaurant preparation (overnight tandoor cooking, house masala, specific butter/cream ratio) may differ from the standardised ICMR-NIN reference preparation, and no Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry exists for this dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) for Dal Makhani (cooked) and supplemented with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175254, cooked black lentils) for micronutrient detail. Values represent a typical restaurant-style preparation with butter and cream. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish, and Dal Makhani is not a significant dietary source of vitamin D. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for this dish. 4. GI of 29 is an analogous estimate based on whole cooked black lentils / urad dal from Atkinson et al. 2021 International GI Tables (peer-reviewed); no direct GI measurement for Dal Makhani exists in approved sources. 5. The 'Khaas (Chef Recommendation)' designation is a restaurant menu qualifier and is not reflected in the canonical food_name, which is standardised to 'Dal Makhani'. 6. food_group is retained as 'Meat and alternatives' per input classification, consistent with HPB Singapore food group taxonomy for legume-based dishes used as protein alternatives. 7. Multilingual names follow the North Indian / Singaporean Indian restaurant context: Hindi (दाल मखनी) is the primary native-script name; Tamil (தால் மக்கனி) and Chinese (黑豆扁豆咖喱) are provided for Singapore multilingual coverage. 8. Serving size of 250 g is based on typical North Indian restaurant bowl portion.
